A committee adopts the proposal and begins sorting explanations through layered filters. The filters are meant to separate strong explanations from weak ones. But the committee also accepts that some historically important ideas first appeared incoherent or low-quality by the standards of their time. So the filters cannot simply reject what looks poor under prevailing standards, because doing that would have excluded at least some later-important ideas. The committee is forced into a tension: either its filters are permissive enough to pass some things that currently seem low-quality, or it risks blocking future breakthroughs.

That tension worsens because “knowledge” is ambiguous. If the filters are supposed to detect knowledge, they need to know what they are detecting. A plausible conjecture may pass earlier than a true explanation. A novel explanation may fail if novelty is not the criterion. A criticism-resistant explanation may survive scrutiny even if it is not yet true, while a true explanation may initially lack resistance because critics misunderstand it. Since these are not obviously the same thing, decomposition into filters cannot be cleanly evaluated. A system tuned for plausibility may suppress novelty; one tuned for truth may be unusable before evidence arrives; one tuned for criticism-resistance may reward rhetorical durability rather than explanatory power.

The Einstein premise narrows one issue and leaves another open. If relativity required no new words, then vocabulary is not a necessary barrier to novelty. A person may state a transformative theory using familiar language. So any filter that treats absence of new terminology as evidence against originality is unreliable. But this does not establish the converse: recombining old words may still fail to produce genuinely new explanatory content. Therefore, linguistic familiarity neither disqualifies nor guarantees novelty. The committee cannot use wording alone as a dependable proxy.

Meanwhile, in the same world, war is abolished. Some people faint from amazement at this abolition. Later, a truce is produced, and this amazes those same people. Their amazement implies that “truce” still has intelligible force even after war has been abolished. So either the abolition is incomplete, recent, reversible, local, or conceptualized differently from the condition a truce addresses. At minimum, the social vocabulary has outlasted a clean one-to-one match with circumstances. Old words remain in circulation under altered conditions, much as Einstein’s case showed old words can persist through conceptual change.

Also in this world, a sensible stewardess hears slamming from a secluded crypt. Since she is sensible, the hearing is not casually dismissible as whim. Since the crypt is secluded, the sound comes from somewhere not normally in open contact. That introduces a concrete anomaly: some event is occurring in an isolated place and is nevertheless perceptible.

Taken together, the world contains systems of judgment that cannot rely on surface coherence, cannot rely on a settled definition of knowledge, cannot rely on new vocabulary as a marker of novelty, and must still respond to startling events—political and physical—that exceed tidy classification.
